title: The Real Number System
blurb: Rational vs. irrational numbers, terminating and repeating decimals, classifying the real numbers, and placing any number on the line.

lessons.title: Rational Numbers
lessons.concreteComparison: Slice a pizza into 8 equal pieces and take 3 of them. You just wrote a number as a ratio: $\frac{3}{8}$. Any number you can write as one whole number over another — a **ratio** — is called a **rational** number. Fractions, decimals you can pin down, and even plain whole numbers all live in this family.
lessons.objective: Recognize a rational number as any number that can be written as a ratio $\frac{a}{b}$ of two integers (with $b\neq 0$), and see why integers and terminating decimals qualify.
concept: A **rational number** is any number that can be written as a fraction $\frac{a}{b}$, where $a$ and $b$ are integers and $b\neq 0$. The word **rational** comes from **ratio**.
Every integer is rational: $5 = \frac{5}{1}$ and $-7 = \frac{-7}{1}$. You just put it over $1$.
Terminating decimals are rational too. $0.75 = \frac{75}{100} = \frac{3}{4}$, and $0.6 = \frac{6}{10} = \frac{3}{5}$.
The denominator can never be $0$ — dividing by zero has no meaning, so $\frac{a}{0}$ is never a number.
lessons.keyIdea: If you can write it as $\frac{\text{integer}}{\text{integer}}$ (bottom not zero), it's rational.

facts.title: Terminating decimals are rational
facts.explanation: Read the place value, then simplify.

workedExamples.statement: Write $0.75$ as a fraction in lowest terms.
Show that $-6$ is rational by writing it as a ratio.
Write $0.35$ as a fraction in lowest terms.

commonMistakes.correction: Integers and terminating decimals are rational too — $5=\frac{5}{1}$ and $0.5=\frac{1}{2}$.
$\frac{a}{b}$ is only rational when $b\neq 0$; division by zero is undefined.

tryIt.statement: Write $0.4$ as a fraction in lowest terms.
Write $12$ as a ratio of two integers.

steps.title: Ratio of two integers
beats: A **rational** number is a ratio $\frac{a}{b}$ of two integers.

steps.title: Integers are rational
beats: Every integer is secretly a fraction.
Put it over $1$ and the ratio rule is satisfied.

steps.title: Is it rational?
steps.prompt: Which of these is **not** automatically written as a ratio of integers yet?
steps.explanation: All three ARE rational, but $0.75$ needs a step: $0.75=\frac{3}{4}$. The first two are already ratios.

steps.title: Turning decimals into ratios
steps.intro: Read the place value, then simplify.

examples.prompt: Write $0.6$ as a fraction in lowest terms.
Write $0.25$ as a fraction in lowest terms.

steps.title: Rational or not?
steps.intro: Every one of these can be written as a ratio.

problems.prompt: Write $0.8$ as a fraction in lowest terms.
Write $0.05$ as a fraction in lowest terms.

steps.title: Why “rational”?
steps.body: The word has nothing to do with being sensible. It comes straight from **ratio** — a comparison of two whole amounts. Ancient Greek mathematicians believed **every** length was a ratio of whole numbers… until one number broke that belief. You'll meet it soon.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **rational** number is a ratio $\frac{a}{b}$ of integers, $b\neq 0$.

lessons.title: Terminating & Repeating Decimals
lessons.concreteComparison: Type $1\div 3$ into a calculator and it spills out $0.3333\ldots$ forever. Type $3\div 8$ and it stops cleanly at $0.375$. Both come from fractions, so both are rational — one just **terminates** while the other **repeats**. Every rational number does exactly one of these two things.
lessons.objective: Convert fractions to decimals by dividing, tell terminating from repeating decimals, and see that every rational number is one or the other.
concept: To turn a fraction into a decimal, **divide the top by the bottom**. $\frac{3}{8} = 3\div 8 = 0.375$.
A **terminating** decimal stops: $0.375$, $0.5$, $0.24$. A **repeating** decimal has a block that goes on forever: $\frac{1}{3}=0.333\ldots$, $\frac{2}{11}=0.1818\ldots$
We mark the repeating block with a bar: $0.\overline{3}$ means $0.333\ldots$, and $0.\overline{18}$ means $0.181818\ldots$
Here's the big fact: **every rational number is either terminating or repeating.** Long division can only produce so many different remainders, so eventually the pattern must stop or cycle.
lessons.keyIdea: Every rational number, written as a decimal, either terminates or repeats — never random forever.

workedExamples.statement: Is $\frac{1}{3}$ terminating or repeating? Check that $3\times\frac{1}{3}=1$.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking a repeating decimal is irrational.
commonMistakes.correction: $0.\overline{3}=\frac{1}{3}$ is perfectly rational — repeating is fine, as long as it repeats a pattern.

commonMistakes.text: Rounding a repeating decimal and calling it exact.

steps.title: Fraction to decimal
beats: To convert $\frac{a}{b}$ to a decimal, compute $a\div b$.

steps.title: Terminate or repeat
beats: We write the repeat with a bar: $0.\overline{3}$.
There is no third option for a fraction.

steps.title: Dividing it out
steps.intro: Divide top by bottom and watch what happens.

problems.prompt: Which fraction gives a repeating decimal?

steps.title: A famous surprise
steps.body: Here's something that feels wrong but is true: $0.\overline{9} = 1$. Exactly. Since $\frac{1}{3}=0.\overline{3}$, multiply both sides by $3$: $1 = 0.\overline{9}$. A repeating $9$ forever **is** one whole. Repeating decimals hide neat secrets.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Convert a fraction by dividing top by bottom.

lessons.title: Irrational Numbers
lessons.concreteComparison: Draw a square one unit on each side and stretch a string across its diagonal. How long is that string? It's $\sqrt{2}=1.41421356\ldots$ — a decimal that never stops and never repeats. No fraction of whole numbers hits it exactly. Numbers like this are **irrational**, and they were a genuine shock when first discovered.
lessons.objective: Recognize an irrational number as one that cannot be written as a ratio of integers — its decimal never terminates and never repeats — and identify common examples like $\sqrt{2}$ and $\pi$.
concept: An **irrational** number **cannot** be written as $\frac{a}{b}$ for any integers. Its decimal goes on forever with **no repeating pattern**.
The square root of any whole number that isn't a perfect square is irrational: $\sqrt{2}$, $\sqrt{3}$, $\sqrt{5}$, $\sqrt{10}$. But $\sqrt{9}=3$ is rational — $9$ is a perfect square.
The most famous irrational is $\pi = 3.14159\ldots$, the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter. It never terminates or repeats.
Careful: $\pi \approx \frac{22}{7}$ is only an **approximation**. $\frac{22}{7}=3.142857\ldots$ is close, but not equal to $\pi$.
lessons.keyIdea: Irrational = never terminates, never repeats, and can't be written as a fraction of integers.

facts.title: Non-perfect square roots
facts.explanation: Irrational unless the number is a perfect square.

workedExamples.solution: $\sqrt{9}=3=\frac{3}{1}$, so it's rational — $9$ is a perfect square.
$1^2=1$ and $2^2=4$, and $2$ is between them, so $1<\sqrt{2}<2$.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking every square root is irrational.
commonMistakes.correction: $\sqrt{9}=3$ and $\sqrt{16}=4$ are rational; only non-perfect squares give irrationals.
$\frac{22}{7}$ is just a close approximation. $\pi$ is irrational and equals no fraction.

tryIt.statement: Between which two whole numbers does $\sqrt{5}$ lie?

steps.title: Never ending, never repeating
beats: An **irrational** number can't be written as $\frac{a}{b}$.
Its decimal runs forever with **no pattern**.

steps.title: Perfect squares stay rational
beats: Rule: root of a **non**-perfect square is irrational.

steps.title: Rational root?
steps.explanation: $\sqrt{9}=3$. The others are non-perfect squares, so irrational.

steps.title: Trapping a root between integers
steps.intro: Find the perfect squares on either side.

problems.explanation: $3$ is not a perfect square, so $\sqrt{3}$ is irrational.
$\pi$ never terminates or repeats; the others are rational.

steps.title: The number that broke the rules
steps.body: Legend says the Greek who proved $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational — that it can't be any fraction — was cast off a ship for it. The Pythagoreans believed the universe was built from whole-number ratios, and this one number shattered that belief. Today we know irrationals **outnumber** rationals infinitely.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Roots of perfect squares ($\sqrt{9}=3$) stay rational.

lessons.title: Classifying Real Numbers
lessons.concreteComparison: Think of numbers like nested boxes. The smallest box holds the counting numbers $1,2,3$. A bigger box adds $0$ and the negatives. A bigger one still adds all the fractions. And the biggest box — the **real numbers** — throws in the irrationals like $\sqrt{2}$ and $\pi$. Every number belongs to one or more of these boxes.
lessons.objective: Sort numbers into the nested sets — natural, whole, integer, rational, irrational — and see how they fit inside the real numbers.
concept: **Natural numbers**: the counting numbers $1,2,3,\ldots$ **Whole numbers**: the naturals plus $0$. **Integers**: whole numbers and their negatives $\ldots,-2,-1,0,1,2,\ldots$
**Rational numbers** include every integer plus all fractions and terminating/repeating decimals. **Irrational numbers** are the ones that aren't rational, like $\sqrt{2}$ and $\pi$.
Together, the rationals and irrationals make up the **real numbers** — every number on the number line.

The sets nest: every natural number is whole, every whole number is an integer, every integer is rational, and every rational is real. But rational and irrational never overlap — a number is one or the other, never both.
lessons.keyIdea: Naturals ⊂ Wholes ⊂ Integers ⊂ Rationals ⊂ Reals; irrationals fill the rest of the reals.

facts.title: The nesting
facts.explanation: Each set sits inside the next.

facts.title: No overlap
facts.explanation: A real number is exactly one of the two.

workedExamples.solution: $-4$ is an integer and rational (and real), since $-4=\frac{-4}{1}$. It is not whole or natural.
$\sqrt{2}$ is irrational and real; it is not rational. Note $\sqrt{2}\cdot\sqrt{2}=2$.
$7$ is natural, whole, integer, rational, and real.

commonMistakes.correction: $0$ is a whole number and an integer, but the naturals start at $1$.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking a number can be both rational and irrational.
commonMistakes.correction: Every real number is exactly one of the two — the sets never overlap.

tryIt.solution: No. $-3$ is an integer and rational, but whole numbers are $0,1,2,\ldots$

steps.title: Each set sits inside the next
beats: Every natural number is also whole.
Every whole number is also an integer.
Every rational — and every irrational — is real.

steps.title: Name that number
steps.prompt: Which is the **most complete** description of $-5$?
steps.explanation: $-5$ is negative, so not natural or whole; it is an integer and rational.

steps.title: Full classification
steps.intro: List every family a number belongs to.

problems.prompt: Which set does $0$ belong to but the naturals don't include it?
problems.explanation: $0$ is whole and an integer, but not natural.
$\frac{2}{3}$ is a ratio of integers → rational.

steps.title: Bigger than infinity?
steps.body: There are infinitely many rational numbers — but infinitely **more** irrationals. Mathematician Georg Cantor proved the irrationals are a “bigger” infinity than the rationals. If you threw a dart at the number line, you'd almost certainly hit an irrational.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Irrationals fill the rest of the real line.

lessons.title: Comparing & Ordering Real Numbers
lessons.concreteComparison: You're comparing prices: is $\frac{3}{4}$ of a dollar more than $0.7$? To line up numbers of different types — fractions, decimals, and roots — the trick is to convert them to a common form (usually decimals) so you can see which is truly bigger. Even an irrational like $\sqrt{2}$ can be pinned between decimals and ordered.
lessons.objective: Compare and order rational and irrational numbers by converting to a common form and estimating roots.
concept: To compare numbers, put them in the **same form** — decimals work well. $\frac{3}{4}=0.75$ and $0.75>0.7$, so $\frac{3}{4}>0.7$.
To place an irrational root, trap it between whole numbers. $\sqrt{50}$: since $7^2=49$ and $8^2=64$, we get $7<\sqrt{50}<8$, and it's just above $7$.
For a finer estimate, test decimals: $7.1^2=50.41$, which is already past $50$, so $\sqrt{50}\approx 7.07$.
Once everything is a decimal (exact or estimated), order from least to greatest just like a number line.
lessons.keyIdea: Convert to a common form, estimate roots between perfect squares, then order on the line.

commonMistakes.text: Comparing a fraction and a decimal without converting.
commonMistakes.correction: Put them in the same form first: $\frac{3}{4}=0.75$, then compare.
A square root is much smaller than the number — $\sqrt{50}\approx 7.07$, not $25$.

steps.title: Same form, then compare
beats: Convert everything to one form — usually decimals.
For roots, estimate between perfect squares.

steps.title: Squeeze between squares
beats: Now it can be ordered with any decimal.

steps.title: Close, but no cigar
steps.body: For centuries people hunted better fractions for $\pi$: $\frac{22}{7}$, then $\frac{355}{113}$ (correct to 6 decimals!). None is exact, because $\pi$ is irrational. But $\frac{355}{113}$ is so good it was used in ancient China over a thousand years ago.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Convert to a common form (decimals) to compare.
Trap a root between perfect squares to estimate.
Then order everything on the number line.

lessons.title: The Real Number Line
lessons.concreteComparison: Picture a ruler with no gaps — not just the whole-inch marks, but every point in between. That's the **real number line**: every real number, rational or irrational, has exactly one home on it, and every point on it is some real number. There are no holes. $\sqrt{2}$ sits at a real spot, a hair past $1.41$.
lessons.objective: See that every real number corresponds to a point on the number line, locate irrationals by estimating, and understand that the reals fill the line completely.
concept: The **real number line** matches every real number to exactly one point, and every point to exactly one real number. Rationals and irrationals together leave no gaps.
To place an irrational, estimate it. $\sqrt{2}\approx 1.41$ sits between $1.4$ and $1.5$, closer to $1.4$. $\pi\approx 3.14$ sits just past $3$.
Between any two real numbers there's always another — in fact, infinitely many. The reals are **dense**: no matter how close two numbers are, more numbers hide between them.
This is why we can always find a decimal approximation as accurate as we like: we just zoom in on the line.
lessons.keyIdea: Every real number is a point on the line, every point is a real number, and there are no gaps.

facts.title: Place by estimating
facts.explanation: Put irrationals near their decimal value.

workedExamples.statement: Where does $\sqrt{2}$ sit relative to $1.4$ and $1.5$?
Where does $\sqrt{8}$ sit between two whole numbers?

commonMistakes.text: Thinking irrationals don't fit on the line.
commonMistakes.correction: They do — $\sqrt{2}$ and $\pi$ each mark one exact point; we just estimate to draw them.

commonMistakes.text: Believing there's a 'next' real number after a given one.
commonMistakes.correction: There isn't — between any two reals lie infinitely many more. The line is dense.

tryIt.statement: Between which two tenths does $\sqrt{2}$ fall?
Estimate $\sqrt{18}$ to the nearest whole number.

steps.title: Every point is a real number
beats: The **real number line** has no gaps.
Every real number sits at exactly one point.
Every point stands for exactly one real number.
Rationals and irrationals together fill it completely.

steps.title: Estimate, then mark
beats: Use the decimal value to find the spot.
The mark is exact even though we round to draw it.

steps.title: Locating numbers
steps.intro: Estimate the value, then place it.

examples.prompt: Between which whole numbers does $\pi$ sit?
Between which whole numbers does $\sqrt{8}$ sit?

problems.prompt: How many real numbers lie between $1$ and $2$?
problems.explanation: The reals are dense — infinitely many between any two.

steps.title: Zoom forever
steps.body: The real line is **infinitely detailed**. Pick any tiny stretch — say between $1.414$ and $1.415$ — and it still holds infinitely many numbers, including $\sqrt{2}$. You can zoom in forever and never run out of room. That endless density is what makes the reals 'continuous.'

steps.title: You've mapped the reals
steps.body: You can now sort every number into its family, estimate irrationals, and place any real number on the line.

steps.title: You've finished “The Real Number System”
steps.body: You can tell rational from irrational, convert and compare across forms, and locate any number on the line. Next up: **Exponents & Scientific Notation**, where a handful of rules tame huge and tiny numbers.

practice.statement: Write $0.45$ as a fraction in lowest terms.

testYourself.statement: Write $0.16$ as a fraction in lowest terms.
Is $0.\overline{6}$ rational? Justify with a product.
